title: Money Pulse · 2026-05-31
intro: What journalists, analysts, and people on X are saying about money this week — summarized by our desk with links to originals.

sections.topic: stock market and index investing this week
sections.headline: Asian Markets Hit Records on Tech Rally While Iran Truce Hopes Drive U.S. Stocks to Banner Month
sections.summary: Japanese and South Korean equities scaled fresh record highs following Wall Street's technology-driven surge, with the S&P 500 notching its ninth consecutive weekly gain. Reports of a potential U.S.-Iran ceasefire extension agreement fueled optimism, though geopolitical uncertainty kept broader sentiment cautious as May concluded with the Nasdaq up 8.4% for the month.
sections.editorial:
Global equity markets closed May on a high note, with [Asian bourses following Wall Street's lead](https://www.investing.com/news/stock-market-news/asia-stocks-japan-skorea-hit-records-on-wall-st-tech-rally-iran-fears-persist-4711171) as technology stocks powered indices to unprecedented levels. South Korean chipmaker SK Hynix surged 9.31% while Samsung added 2.68%, reflecting the semiconductor industry's continued momentum. The rally wasn't uniform across the region—China's CSI300 dipped 0.80%—but Japanese and Korean markets demonstrated exceptional strength as investors bet on sustained AI-driven growth. [Dell Technologies delivered a particularly powerful performance](https://www.investors.com/market-trend/the-big-picture/dow-jones-stock-market-nasdaq-sp500-trump-iran-ceasefire-dell/) as enterprise AI spending showed no signs of cooling.

The geopolitical backdrop added complexity to an otherwise bullish narrative. [Reports that U.S. and Iranian negotiators reached a truce deal](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/30/3-themes-that-drove-stocks-to-another-record-week-and-a-banner-month.html) pending President Trump's approval sent the S&P 500 and Nasdaq to fresh records on Thursday, though the conflict that began February 28 continues to cast shadows over sustained risk appetite. Market veterans have counseled against overreacting to individual headlines amid the volatility. Meanwhile, [the S&P 500's nine-week winning streak](https://www.investopedia.com/stock-market-today-dow-jones-s-and-p-500-05292026-11986232) represents a remarkable technical achievement, with the Nasdaq's 8.4% monthly gain underscoring the concentrated nature of recent outperformance. Oil suffered its biggest monthly decline even as [energy markets processed the Iran developments](https://www.wsj.com/livecoverage/stock-market-today-dow-sp-500-nasdaq-05-29-2026), highlighting the complex cross-currents investors are navigating.

socialHighlights.text: Market strategists at BofA identified a historical parallel for the AI rally that diverges from the dot-com boom comparison many investors have drawn.
The number of 401(k) millionaires declined despite workers hitting record savings rates, reflecting how market concentration affected retirement account balances during the period.
Analysts noted that Micron investors are celebrating like it's 1987, yet valuation metrics suggest the semiconductor stock still trades at reasonable multiples relative to earnings potential.

sections.headline: Household Budgets Buckle as Inflation Hits Multi-Year High, Consumer Spending Stalls
sections.summary: America's inflation gauge reached its highest point since 2023, with core PCE rising 3.3% annually while real consumer spending barely grew 0.1% in April. Middle-income families face even steeper price increases—up 4.4% on necessities like food and gas—forcing shoppers to prioritize value retailers and scale back discretionary purchases as economic strain signals mount across households.
sections.editorial:
The collision of accelerating inflation and weakening purchasing power is reshaping American consumer behavior in fundamental ways. [PCE inflation climbed to 3.8% year-over-year](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-28/us-consumer-spending-barely-rises-as-inflation-accelerates), while inflation-adjusted spending crawled forward just 0.1%, revealing consumers' diminishing ability to maintain their standard of living. For middle-income households, the squeeze is even tighter—[Primerica's adjusted index shows inflation running at 4.4%](https://markets.ft.com/data/announce/detail?dockey=600-202605280000BIZWIRE_USPRX____20260527_BW431413-1) when focused on necessities these families actually buy. "Signs of stress are building inside the American household across the economy," RSM chief economist Joe Brusuelas [told the Los Angeles Times](https://www.latimes.com/business/story/2026-05-28/key-inflation-gauge-worsens-as-americans-income-spending-power-erodes), pointing to eroding disposable income and inflation-adjusted spending as harbingers of further slowdown.

Retail earnings paint a vivid picture of this budget recalibration in action. Thursday results from Dollar Tree, Burlington, Best Buy, and Kohl's demonstrate that [shoppers remain willing to spend—but only on bargains and small indulgences](https://www.axios.com/2026/05/28/retail-earnings-costco-kohls-best-buy-gap), according to Axios. [CBS News identified five key stress signals](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/credit-card-delinquencies-savings-rate-us-economy/) including rising credit card delinquencies and falling savings rates, while [consumer confidence slides](https://www.greenwichtime.com/business/article/america-in-focus-inflation-gauge-hits-multiyear-22283830.php) amid the painful reality of grocery store and gas station visits. This behavioral shift toward value-seeking and necessity prioritization suggests the American consumer—long the economy's most reliable engine—is running on fumes, with implications that extend far beyond retail into broader economic growth prospects.

socialHighlights.text: When inflation rises, consumer purchasing power drops and business margins shrink, leading to heavy market volatility that requires strategic responses.
For most Americans, inflation determines the difference between saving versus spending, vacationing versus staying home—fundamental lifestyle trade-offs.

sections.topic: credit card rewards interest rates Fed
sections.headline: Credit Card Sector Faces Mounting Pressure as Debt Soars, Rates Stay High, and Regulatory Scrutiny Intensifies
sections.summary: American credit card debt has reached $1.25 trillion while average APRs remain stubbornly above 20%, creating a financial squeeze that's pushing consumers toward cheaper alternatives like personal loans. Meanwhile, regulatory proposals including rate caps and interchange fee reforms threaten to fundamentally reshape the rewards ecosystem that millions of cardholders depend on.
sections.editorial:
The credit card industry stands at a critical inflection point as multiple pressures converge. Despite [Federal Reserve rate cuts](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/will-credit-card-interest-rates-drop-soon-experts-weigh-in), credit card APRs have barely budged from historic highs above 21%, according to Fed data, while [Americans are falling behind on $1.25 trillion in credit card debt](https://www.wsj.com/personal-finance/credit/us-credit-card-debt-af5c7c77). The disconnect reflects issuers' cautious approach in an uncertain economy—CardRatings found that just over half of studied cards lowered rates after the Fed's Q4 2024 cuts. This rate stickiness is driving debt-burdened consumers toward [personal loans with rates averaging 12.04%](https://www.investopedia.com/drowning-in-high-credit-card-rates-americans-turn-to-a-cheaper-lifeline-11947396), nearly 10 percentage points below credit card averages, while [savings rates have fallen to 2022 lows](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/28/savings-inflation-americans-financial-stress.html) as inflation continues outpacing paychecks.

Beyond market dynamics, political intervention looms large over the sector's future structure. [Senator Warren is scrutinizing Bilt's rewards card relaunch](https://www.wsj.com/finance/regulation/sen-warren-asks-bilt-for-data-on-rocky-relaunch-of-rewards-for-rent-card-3dbe8478), signaling heightened regulatory attention on consumer protection. More significantly, proposals for [10% interest rate caps](https://www.aei.org/research-products/report/should-credit-card-interest-rates-be-capped-at-10-percent) and the pending Credit Card Competition Act threaten to fundamentally alter the economics of rewards programs. Industry experts warn that rate caps could lead to "[diminished rewards, points and miles](https://www.investopedia.com/trump-s-credit-card-interest-rate-cap-could-mean-diminished-rewards-points-and-miles-11883929)" as banks adjust to compressed margins—a pattern similar to the Durbin Amendment's impact on debit cards, which [reduced free checking availability by 35%](https://www.nerdwallet.com/credit-cards/news/is-congress-going-to-kill-credit-card-rewards) and raised monthly fees by 17-20%. Fed research indicates that [transaction-based rewards already exceed net transaction margins](https://www.aei.org/research-products/report/should-credit-card-interest-rates-be-capped-at-10-percent) at major issuers, meaning the current rewards ecosystem depends on interest income and fees that regulatory action would curtail. For the 75 million Americans with rewards cards, these policy debates aren't abstract—they could determine whether premium travel cards and cash-back programs remain economically viable for mass-market issuers.

socialHighlights.text: CardRatings executive editor Jennifer Doss notes that credit card companies remain cautious about lowering rates because when the Fed cuts during economic slowdowns, lending to consumers becomes riskier.

Industry analyst Todd Funk warns that proposed rate caps could lead to fewer card products with higher annual fees targeting narrower customer segments, as banks aren't going to lose money if they can avoid it.

sections.headline: U.S. Mortgage Rates Hover Near 6.5% as Housing Market Shifts Toward Buyer Advantage
sections.summary: Mortgage rates have climbed to a nine-month high of 6.53-6.56% amid geopolitical uncertainty, pushing refinancing applications down 18% and purchase loans to a 12-year low. Despite elevated borrowing costs, rising inventory and cooling price growth are giving buyers newfound leverage in a market that's dramatically shifted from recent years.
sections.editorial:
The American housing market is experiencing a paradoxical moment: [mortgage rates have surged to 6.53%](https://www.realtor.com/news/trends/mortgage-interest-rates-now-may-28-2026/) despite hopes that an Iran peace deal might stabilize financial markets, while simultaneously [home purchase loans have plunged to their lowest level in 12 years](https://www.realtor.com/news/trends/home-purchase-loans-12-year-low-attom-report-q1-2026/). The National Association of Realtors reports that [pending home sales increased 1.4% month-over-month in April](https://www.thestreet.com/real-estate/real-estate-small-business-investing-data-2026), suggesting demand persists even as affordability deteriorates. [Refinancing applications dropped 18%](https://www.realtor.com/news/trends/mortgage-applications-purchase-refinance-today-may-27-2026/) as rate-sensitive borrowers retreat, highlighting how quickly market dynamics can shift when borrowing costs rise.

Yet beneath these sobering headlines lies a more nuanced story for prospective buyers. [Rising inventory and flattening asking prices are creating negotiating leverage](https://www.realtor.com/news/real-estate-news/weeky-housing-market-update-may-29-video/) not seen in years, as sellers confront a reality where homes sit longer on the market and multiple-bid frenzies fade. Financial advisors are increasingly counseling clients to [focus on personal readiness rather than rate timing](https://www.investopedia.com/even-with-rates-rising-mortgages-are-still-cheaper-now-than-they-were-the-last-2-springs-11981354), noting that today's 6.5% rates remain below the levels of spring 2023 and 2024. The median home price has reached nearly $420,000 nationally, making the [rent-versus-buy calculation genuinely complicated](https://www.monarch.com/blog/renting-versus-buying-pros-cons-and-considerations) in a way it hasn't been for a generation—but those with stable finances and long-term horizons may find this transitional moment offers opportunities that disappear once rates inevitably decline and competition reignites.

socialHighlights.text: NerdWallet analysts note that after mortgage rates fell to their lowest point since September 2022 in February, they spiked upward in March as the Iran war stoked inflation concerns.
Investopedia financial advisors are telling clients that trying to time the perfect rate lock is nearly impossible, recommending buyers focus on financial readiness rather than rate predictions.
Monarch's financial planning team observes that with 30-year fixed mortgages hovering around 6.5% and median home prices near $420,000, the financial calculus of buying is genuinely complicated.
